title: Memory Leaks
breadcrumbs: Development Considerations for Performance and Scalability
text: Memory Leaks Detecting real memory leaks in complex Uniface applications can be difficult. One indication that a problem exists is that the memory usage continues to grow when repeating exactly the same set of actions. (You can monitor the memory usage with, for example, the Performance Monitor tab of the Windows Task Manager or by using ps-efl on Unix.) Running the same form with the same data many times is one such example. If the test case is more complex, particularly if it involves multiple users running operations in the application server, it might be necessary to repeat the test case many times before the memory usage stabilizes. Try to reduce the complexity of the test case and run it only with a single user. If the memory usage still continues to grow, there is probably a memory leak. If you suspect a memory leak, and you are an expert user, you can also use a third-party tool to help identify a memory problem. However, because of the nature of the Uniface memory manager, reported problems might not be real problems, and not every potential problem can be identified. Not all memory leaks are serious problems, because the operating system completely returns all memory that was allocated to a program when that program terminates. However, in non-terminating programs (such as Uniface Router or an asynchronous Uniface Server), even a small leak can cause a problem. Consider the following when assessing the severity of the problem: If the leak is of a small, fixed size during the entire lifetime of a program, the negative effect is limited. Stability will not be affected and the performance implications are minimal. If the leak is small (a few bytes), it can be a relatively harmless problem, depending on where and how often it occurs. For example, a leak of a few bytes per form in the client part of an application is unlikely to cause a problem, because even an application consisting of hundreds of forms will only use a small additional amount of memory. However, a leak of the same size occurring in the application server for each activate can be more serious, because it can cause the server to reach the limit of available memory and stop. If the leak is very large (several kilobytes), it is possible that the stability or performance of the application is adversely affected. Such a problem should be reported to Uniface for further investigation. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
